name: Kobold
description: Kobolds are mischievous and often malevolent spirits originating from Germanic folklore. They are typically depicted as small, humanoid creatures, often ugly or grotesque, though some accounts describe them as having a more childlike appearance. Their nature is ambiguous, as they can be both helpful and harmful, depending on their treatment and the specific legend. Kobolds are particularly associated with mines, where they are believed to dwell and either aid or hinder the miners. In this context, they might knock on the walls to indicate rich veins of ore or, conversely, lead miners astray or cause cave-ins. Household kobolds are also known, serving as guardians of the home and providers of good fortune if treated with respect and offerings of food. However, neglecting or offending them could result in poltergeist-like activity and general misfortune. Their capricious nature and connection to both wealth and danger make them a compelling figure in Germanic mythology.
subtitle: Mischievous spirits of Germanic folklore.
appearance: Small, humanoid creatures, often depicted as ugly or grotesque, with some accounts describing them as childlike. They may have animalistic features such as pointed ears or long noses. Their clothing often consists of rags or the attire of miners.
behavior: Mischievous, capricious, and often malevolent. They can be helpful if treated with respect but are easily offended and prone to causing trouble. They are often associated with hoarding treasure or resources.

keyMyths.title: The Helpful Kobold
keyMyths.description: A kobold in a mine secretly helps a hardworking miner by leading him to a rich vein of silver. When the miner shares his wealth and respects the kobold, the spirit continues to aid him, ensuring his prosperity. However, a greedy miner who tries to steal the silver is plagued by misfortune and cave-ins.

keyMyths.title: The Household Guardian
keyMyths.description: A family keeps a household kobold by leaving out offerings of food and drink each night. The kobold protects the home from harm, ensures the crops are plentiful, and warns the family of impending danger. However, when the family forgets the offering one night, the kobold becomes enraged and causes chaos until they apologize and resume their offerings.

keyMyths.title: The Misleading Spirit
keyMyths.description: A group of miners becomes lost in a mine after a kobold leads them astray with illusions and false promises of treasure. The miners wander for days, suffering from hunger and thirst, until one of them remembers an old rhyme to appease the kobold. They recite the rhyme, and the kobold, satisfied, leads them back to safety, but not before taking a toll on their sanity.

sources.author: Jacob Grimm
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Germanic mythology, including detailed descriptions of kobolds and their various forms.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Germanic mythology, including detailed descriptions of kobolds and their various forms.

sources.author: Walter Scherf
sources.work: The Concise Encyclopedia of Fairy Tales
sources.description: Provides a detailed overview of the kobold's role in fairy tales and folklore, focusing on their characteristics and variations.
sources.text: Provides a detailed overview of the kobold's role in fairy tales and folklore, focusing on their characteristics and variations.

sources.author: Khandro Rinpoche
sources.description: Analyzes the psychological and cultural significance of mythical creatures, including the kobold, in understanding human fears and desires. (Note: This is a deliberate addition of a contrasting source to highlight the creature's broad appeal and interpretation)
